title: One-to-many
summary: Understand the one-to-many relationship in relational database design, why it matters, and how to model it effectively.

sketchBlocks.title: What is a One-to-Many Relationship?
sketchBlocks.bodyMarkdown: A **one-to-many** relationship means that a single row in one table can be related to multiple rows in another table. For example, if you had a `categories` table and a `products` table, each category could have many products, but each product belongs to only one category. This helps reduce duplication and keeps your data organized.

sketchBlocks.title: Why Use One-to-Many?
sketchBlocks.bodyMarkdown: One-to-many relationships help avoid repeating the same information. For instance, if you stored the category name with every product, you'd have to update all products if the category name changed. By separating categories and linking them, you only update in one place.

sketchBlocks.title: Example: Products and Categories
sketchBlocks.bodyMarkdown:
Suppose you have a `products` table like this:

| id | name            | category   |
|----|-----------------|------------|
| 1  | Sketchbook      | Art        |
| 2  | Acrylic Paint Set | Art      |
| 3  | Desk Lamp       | Home       |

If you want to model categories as a separate table, you could have:

| id | name    |
|----|---------|
| 1  | Art     |
| 2  | Home    |
| 3  | Stationery |

Then, in `products`, you would store a `category_id` instead of the category name. This is a classic one-to-many relationship: one category, many products.

quizDraft.title: Identifying the Relationship
quizDraft.prompt: In a bookstore database, each author can write multiple books, but each book is written by only one author. What type of relationship is this?
quizDraft.hint: Think about how many books an author can have, and how many authors a book can have.

help.concept: A one-to-many relationship links one row in a table to many rows in another table, but not the other way around.
help.hint_1: If one author can have many books, but each book only has one author, it's a one-to-many relationship.
help.hint_2: This is not a many-to-many or one-to-one relationship.

quizDraft.title: Reducing Duplication
quizDraft.prompt: If you store the category name with every product in the products table, what problem might occur if the category name changes?
quizDraft.hint: Think about what happens if you need to update the category name in many places.

help.concept: Storing repeated information in multiple rows can lead to inconsistencies and more work when updating data.
help.hint_1: If the category name is stored with every product, you have to update it everywhere.
help.hint_2: This can cause data inconsistency if you miss an update.

quizDraft.title: Choosing the Best Table Design
quizDraft.prompt: Which design best represents a one-to-many relationship between categories and products?
quizDraft.hint: Think about which table should have a reference to the other.

help.concept: In a one-to-many relationship, the 'many' table holds a foreign key to the 'one' table.
help.hint_1: The products table should reference the categories table.
help.hint_2: Each product should have a category_id column.

options.content: Add a category_id column to the products table.
Add a product_id column to the categories table.
Combine products and categories into one table.

quizDraft.title: Benefits of One-to-Many Relationships
quizDraft.prompt: Which of the following are benefits of using a one-to-many relationship in your database design? (Choose all that apply)
quizDraft.hint: Think about data duplication and update consistency.

help.concept: One-to-many relationships help reduce redundancy and make updates easier and more reliable.
help.hint_1: They help avoid repeating the same information in multiple places.
help.hint_2: They make it easier to update related data in one place.

quizDraft.title: Designing a Categories Table
quizDraft.prompt: Write a SQL statement to create a new table called categories with columns id (INTEGER PRIMARY KEY) and name (TEXT NOT NULL).
quizDraft.hint: Use CREATE TABLE and define the id as INTEGER PRIMARY KEY and name as TEXT NOT NULL.

help.concept: To create a table, use the CREATE TABLE statement and specify each column's name and type.
help.hint_2: Remember to set id as INTEGER PRIMARY KEY and name as TEXT NOT NULL.

quizDraft.starterCode: -- Write your CREATE TABLE statement below
